coredata.dc:title: A novel mitochondria-targeted fluorescent probe based on carbon dots for Cu2+ imaging in living cells and zebrafish
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Qing
Deng, Sha
Jin, Liying
Jiang, Yuliang
Jin, Can
Wang, Bingxiang
Shen, Jian
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a novel carbon dots (CDs) was prepared by using citric acid and 1,2-diaminobenzene as raw materials via hydrothermal reaction. The obtained CDs showed a fluorescence turn-off response to divalent copper ion (Cu2+) with high selectively and sensitively which can be observed by the naked eye. The distribution of CDs is spherical with an average diameter about 2.89 nm, as observed by scanning electron microscopy (TEM) which could easily pass through the cell membrane and can be successfully located in mitochondria. In addition, the CDs was successfully applied to imaging Cu2+ in HeLa cells and zebrafish owing to its excellent optical performance and low toxicity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Divalent copper ion
Selectively
Mitochondria
Zebrafish

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The preparation route of carbon dots.
UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs in EtOH /PBS buffer (1:4, v/v, 10 mM, pH 7.4, 37 °C).
TEM image of CDs (a) and (b) particle size distribution of CDs.
Effect of pH (A), NaCl (B), Ex-dependent PL behavior(C) and time (D) on fluorescence intensity of the CDs aqueous solution.
(a) Emission intensity of CDs (10 mM) in the presence of different ions.
(a) Fluorescence change of CDs in the presence of different concentration of Cu2+ (0-200 μM), (b) The relationship of F/F0 vis the concentration of Cu2+ over the range from 0 to 50 μM.

Confocal fluorescence images of HeLa cells. The bright field (a1-a4) and fluorescence image with blue channel(b1-b4), green channel (c1-c4) and merge(d1-d4) of HeLa cells pretreated with 10 mM probe CDs for 30 min and then followed by incubation with 10, 20, 40 μM Cu2+ for another 30 min, respectively. The cells were excited with a light laser at 405 nm. Scale bar: 10 mm.

Intracellular co-localization fluorescence imaging of CDs-Cu2+ and Mito Tracher Green FM in live HeLa cells. Cells incubated with CDs-Cu2+ (10 mM-40 μM) for 30 min and Mito Tracker Green FM (200 nM) for 10 min at 37 °C. (A) Bright-field, (B) Confocal images of CDs-Cu2+ on the blue channel (λex = 405 nm), (C) Fluorescence imaging of Mito Tracker Green FM on the green channel (λex = 488 nm), (D) The merged image of (b) and (c),(E) The normalized intensity profile of the regions of interest across A549 cells. (F) Colocalization scatter plots of (D). PC: 0.74. Scale bar =20 μm.

The bright field (a1-a4) and fluorescence imaging of CDs responding to Cu2+ in zebrafish. (b1-b4) blue channel of zebrafish fed with various concentrations of Cu2+ (10, 20 and 40 μM); (c1-c4) green channel of zebrafish fed CDs with various concentrations of Cu2+; (d1-d4) the merged image of (a1-a4), (b1-b4) and (d1-d4); All scale bars =100 μm.

head.ce:title: A novel mitochondria-targeted fluorescent probe based on carbon dots for Cu2+ imaging in living cells and zebrafish

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanjing Normal Univ, Jiangsu Collaborat Innovat Ctr Biomed Funct Mat, Jiangsu Key Lab Biofunct Mat, Sch Chem & Mat Sci, Nanjing 210023, Jiangsu, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Collaborat Innovat Ctr Biomed Funct Mat

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: a Nanjing Normal Univ, Jiangsu Collaborat Innovat Ctr Biomed Funct Mat, Jiangsu Key Lab Biofunct Mat, Sch Chem & Mat Sci, Nanjing 210023, Jiangsu, Peoples R China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Chemical Industry of Forest Products, Key Lab Biomass Energy & Mater Jiangsu Prov, National Engineering Lab Biomass Chem Utilizat, CAF, Nanjing 210042, Jiangsu, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Chemical Industry of Forest Products
Key Lab Biomass Energy & Mater Jiangsu Prov
National Engineering Lab Biomass Chem Utilizat

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: b Institute of chemical Industry of Forest Products, Key Lab Biomass Energy & Mater Jiangsu Prov, National Engineering Lab Biomass Chem Utilizat, CAF, Nanjing 210042, Jiangsu, Peoples R China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel mitochondria-targeted probe CDs was prepared which could be used to monitor endogenous Cu2+ in HeLa cell and zebrafish.

In this study, a novel carbon dots (CDs) was prepared by using citric acid and 1,2-diaminobenzene as raw materials via hydrothermal reaction. The obtained CDs showed a fluorescence turn-off response to divalent copper ion (Cu2+) with high selectively and sensitively which can be observed by the naked eye. The distribution of CDs is spherical with an average diameter about 2.89 nm, as observed by scanning electron microscopy (TEM) which could easily pass through the cell membrane and can be successfully located in mitochondria. In addition, the CDs was successfully applied to imaging Cu2+ in HeLa cells and zebrafish owing to its excellent optical performance and low toxicity.

ce:section.ce:para: As well known, transition metal ions play an important role in life which involved in many important physiological processes in the organism [1–3]. Copper ion (Cu2+) is a typical transition metal, it is also a necessary trace element in the human body which plays an important role in the development and function of blood, middle pivot nerve, immune system, hair, and a variety of human tissues and organs [4–7]. The total concentration of Cu2+ ion in an adult body to maintain a normal life form is about 100−150 mg, which is the second essential trace element in the human body [8]. However, abnormal levels of Cu2+ concentration will damage the cell, human organ, and brain tissues, furthermore, research showed that some neurodegenerative diseases including Wilson’s disease and Alzheimer’s diseases are related to the abnormal concentration of Cu2+ [9–12]. So, the accurate quantification of Cu2+ concentration is highly significant, particularly for the study of cellular functions and physiological processes which provides clues for early diagnosis and treatment of related diseases.

In view of the important roles of Cu2+ in life, great efforts have been made to develop a variety of methods for Cu2+ detection including atomic absorption spectrometry, plasma emission spectrum, voltammetry, atomic fluorescence spectrometry, and X-ray fluorescence spectrometry [13–17]. Although these techniques improved the detection limit and sensitivity greatly, most of these methods rely on complex operations, high cost and time-consuming procedures which bring great consumption of resources. Therefore, it is of great significance to develop a new method with simple operation, high sensitivity, and rapid response. The appearance of fluorescence analysis has an epoch-making significance that meets all the requirements of modern rapid analysis such as easy operation, simplicity, real-time monitoring, and so on [18–20]. Moreover, the nondestructive testing of the sample allows the tested sample can be reused or further saved. In recent decades, benefiting from the efforts of scientific researchers, fluorescence analysis has been developed rapidly, which facilitates the promotion of environmental and life sciences [21–25].

Crucially, reasonable design of fluorescent probe ensure practical application of fluorescence analysis. So far, numerous fluorescent probes including inorganic quantum dots and organic dyes have been developed and gained great progress in the detection sensitivity and selectivity [26–28]. However, the intrinsic toxicity of inorganic quantum dots and the complex preparation of organic dyes are not in correspondence with the future development direction of fluorescent probes. In recent years, a new type of organic carbon nanomaterials-carbon dots (CDs) appeared and possessed great advantages such as good biocompatibility, excellent photostability, environmental friendliness and easy for modification [29–32] which enable them can be widely utilized in different fields such as fluorescence imaging, photocatalysis, molecular recognition, solar cells, etc. Based on the above considerations, introduce carbon dots in the design of Cu2+ fluorescent probe can not only overcome the defects of traditional fluorescent probes, but also realize the application in the organism by taking advantage of its intrinsic advantages. Herein, a simple and low-cost bottom-up strategy was developed for the facile synthesis of CDs using o-phenylenediamin and citric acid via solvothermal treatment. The aqueous solution of CDs emit blue fluorescence with visible light illumination and showed a fluorescence “turn-off” response to Cu2+ ion with highly selective and sensitive. A good linear relationship was observed between 0−200 μM and the detection limit was calculated to be 3.7 nM, suggesting great potential in a new fluorescent probe for reliable, visualization, and selective detection of Cu2+. In addition, owing to its excellent optical performance, CDs could be applied to imaging Cu2+ in HeLa cells and zebrafish successfully.

Citric acid and 1,2-diaminobenzene were purchased purchased from Adamas-beta® (Shanghai, China). Other testing species such as K+, Ca2+, Ag+, Co2+, Fe3+, Na+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Ni2+ and Cr3+ were purchased from Shanghai Experiment Reagent Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China).

Ultraviolet-Visible (UV–vis) absorption was performed on a Lambda 1050+ spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA). The fluorescence spectra of CDs were measured on F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). 1H NMR experiments were recorded in D2O on an AVANCE III HD AN-400 MHz spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). FTIR spectra were performed by AlphaⅡspectrometer (Bruker, Germany). Cell and in vivo imaging research were observed on an A1 confocal laser scanning microscope (Nikon, Japan). The particle size distribution of carbon dots was obtained by using a JEM-2100 high-resolution transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan).

The preparation route of carbon dots was showed in scheme 1 . Concisely, 5 mmol of citric acid and 10 mmol of 1,2-diaminobenzene were dissolved in 10 mL of distilled water ultrasound for 0.5 h. Subsequently, the mixture was poured into a 100 mL hydrothermal reactor with a Teflon-lined and heated at 200℃ for 6 h. Then, cool naturally to room temperature removed large particles by 0.22 μm filter membrane and then centrifugated at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. The yellow solid powder was obtained after freeze-drying.

HeLa cells were supplied by Cell bank of Chinese Academy of Sciences. First, the methyl thiazolyl tetrazolium (MTT) method was used to estimate the cytotoxicity of CDs in HeLa cells. For imaging, HeLa cells were treated with Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM) with 10 % fetal bovine serum at 37 °C in a humidified incubator with 5% CO2 for 24 h. Four groups of cells were prepared, one group was used as blank control which only incubates with CDs and the other three groups hatched CDs and Cu2+ with various concentrations 10, 20, and 40 μM for another 30 min respectively. The imaging experiment conditions are the same.

Mito-tracker green as a commercial mitochondrial dye be used for assessing the cell localization ability of CDs. Briefly, HeLa cells were co-stained with the probe CDs (10 μM) and Mito-tracker green (200 nM) for 30 min at 37 °C. Then, washed the cells three times by phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) before imaging. Fluorescence images were collected at 405/488 nm for Mito-tracker green.

Zebrafish studies were approved by the Ethics Committee and IACUC of Qilu Health Science Center, Nanjing Normal University, and were conducted in compliance with European guidelines for the care and use of laboratory animals. 5-day-old zebrafish received from the Shanghai FishBio Co., Ltd. Before the experiment, we divided zebrafish into four groups and the first group co-culture with CDs (10 μM) for 30 min at 28 °C as a contrast. Then, after the same operation as above, the other three groups were incubated with 10, 20, and 40 μM Cu2+ for another 30 min respectively. Subsequently, washed with PBS and imaged by Nikon A1 confocal laser scanning microscope with two image channels including a green channel (500–550 nm) and blue channel (425–475 nm).

FT-IR spectroscopy used to estimate the structure of CDs and the results were showed in Fig.S1. CDs sample was found to show characteristic absorption bands including OH at 3425 cm−1, CH at 2850 and 2970 cm−1, stretching vibration of CO at 1621 cm −1, CN at 1450 cm−
1, indicating that o-phenylenediamine can be used as carbon source and nitrogen source for preparing CDs simultaneously. Three typical absorption peaks of CDs local at 243, 273, and 280 nm were observed from UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. 1 ). According to early literatures [33–36], the two absorption peaks at 243 and 273 nm were belong to π-π* transition of the CC and n–π* transition of the CO, respectively, while the absorption peak at 280 nm corresponding to the π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains. The above results also demonstrated that our CDs are rich in abundant hydroxyl and carboxyl groups which potentially possess excellent biocompatibility. In addition, 1H NMR spectrometry was further carried out to confirm the structure of CDs. The peaks located from 7.14 to 8.09 ppm could be ascribed to the typical characteristic of aromatic protons containing nitrogen atoms. The appearance of these signals due to aromatic or olefinic protons demonstrates the presence of sp2 carbons, which suggests the production of CDs in graphite structures (Fig. S2). Moreover, the size and distribution of carbon dots CDs from TEM analysis. As Fig. 2 a and Fig. 2b showed that CDs evenly distributed in aqueous solution and the particle size ranges from 1.1 to 6.5 nm, the average diameter is about 2.89 ± 0.24 nm. In addition, the Raman spectra of the CDs was further studied which showed two peaks at 1370 cm-1 and 1560 cm-1 ascribe to the disordered D band and crystalline G band, respectively (Fig. S3).

The stability property of biomaterials is pivotal for their application in biological systems. Herein, we investigated the stability of the prepared CDs from four influencing aspects including pH, ionic strength, photostability and dependent emission property. Effect of pH value ranging from 2 to 12 on the fluorescence of CDs was firstly studied (Fig. 3 A), the fluorescence of the probe CDs remained stable between pH 4–9 indicating CDs can be used under physiological conditions. Then, the effect of ionic strength on the fluorescence intensity was investigated by changing the concentration of NaCl (Fig. 3B). The fluorescence intensity of CDs decreases slightly as the concentration of NaCl increases from 0 to 1.0 mol/L. The fluorescence signal keeps high even when the concentration of NaCl reaches to 1.0 mol/L, revealing the high fluorescence stability of CDs under high salinity conditions. For the PL spectrum (as shown in Fig. 3C), with the excitation wavelength scanning from 350 nm to 420 nm, an obvious peak locating at around 445 nm is observed, indicating the independence of the emission wavelength of CDs. As seen in Fig. 3D, the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreases slightly even after 10 days. These results demonstrate that the prepared CDs have excellent stability which enables their applications in fluorescent sensing and imaging. The above experimental results showed that probe CDs have good optical properties and possess the prospect of application in the biological systems.

As we know, CDs can be used as an effective electron acceptor to detect metal ions accurately by electron transfer with metal ions. Herein, we first evaluate the effect of CDs on common ions in living organisms. The results are shown in Fig. 4 , compared to the other selected species, only Cu2+ could cause a significant fluorescence turn-off phenomenon. Furthermore, we further carried the interference coexisting experiment and the results conform to the selective experiment (Fig. S4). The above results indicate that the added ions had no obvious influence on the fluorescence of the CDs except for Cu2+.

Benefit from the results of selective experiments, the fluorescence titration project was further used to quantitatively assess the relationship of concentration of Cu2+ and the fluorescence intensity of CDs. The result was showed in Fig. 5 A, with the added Cu2+ from 0 to 50 μM, the fluorescence intensity of CDs at 448 nm decrease gradually, the linear correlation equation is F/F0 = -0.00951 [Cu2+] + 0.99899 with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.99549 (Fig. 5B). Moreover, the detection limit for Cu2+ was estimated to be 3.7 nM which can ensure the accurate detection of trace copper ions in the complex system. We refer to the early literature for the mechanism study [37–39], the plot falls into a nonlinearity relationship while the concentration of Cu2+ between 0 and 200 μM, demonstrating that dynamic quenching effect and static quenching effects is both involved in the fluorescence quenching process. In addition, the zeta potential of CDs was further carried out to be -3.77 mV which can interaction with positively charged Cu2+ effective, of course, this can only be used as a supporting certificate due to there are many other metal ions with positively charged (Fig. S5).

Inspired by the above experimental results, intracellular sensing of Cu2+ was further researched. First, MTT assay was used to assess the toxicity of CDs to HeLa cell and the results were shown in Fig. S4, CDs is almost nontoxic to A549 cells even the concentration of CDs attained 100 μg/mL which was higher than the actually required concentration in this experiment, the apoptosis rate of HeLa cells is still less than 8%. Then, the Cu2+ bioimaging process of CDs with various concentrations (0, 10, 20, 40 μg/mL) was examined by Nikon confocal laser scanning microscope and the results were presented in Fig. 6 , bright fluorescence imaging in blue and green channels can be observed when free CDs incubated living HeLa cells. While various concentration of Cu2+ (10, 20, 40 μg/mL) were further Co-incubated with CDs and HeLa cells for another 8 h, both the channel blue and green fluorescence significantly weakened which testified the detectability of CDs to Cu2+ in living cells.

As an important kind of organelles, mitochondria are the site of many important reactions, therefore, the value of the probe lies in whether it can localize mitochondria. In this paper, a co-localization experiment was carried out by using Mito-Tracker Green FM containing CDs and Cu2+ for mitochondria targeting. Briefly, Mito-Tracker Green FM (mitochondria targeting fluorescent probe) was selected conducted with CDs (10 μM) and Cu2+ (40 μM) in HeLa cells for 30 min then imaging research results showed that our probe CDs and Mito-Tracker green exhibited a good fluorescence imaging overlap phenomenon which proved CDs has the ability of mitochondrial localization in the HeLa cells. (Fig. 7 )

Satisfactory results of imaging and monitoring copper ions in cells based on CDs, we further explore its application in vivo. Zebrafish was selected as a living sample owing to it as a typical vertebrate model that has been widely used in the field of in vivo imaging. Herein, we chose 3-day-old zebrafish as an animal model for estimating the effect of probe CDs in vivo. For specific operations such as cell experiments, zebrafish were divided into four groups. One group only added CDs and co-cultured with zebrafish, the other three groups were further added Cu2+ with concentrations of 10, 20, and 40 μg/mL. After 30 min, fluorescence imaging was performed by laser confocal imaging and the results were shown in Fig.8 , both blue and green channels showed bright fluorescence signal in zebrafish with CDs. While 10–40 μM of Cu2+ were added for another 30 min, both blue and green channels showed obvious fluorescence weakening characteristics which indicating probe CDs can imaging and monitoring Cu2+ in vivo and provide information for early diagnosis of related diseases.

In summary, a novel CDs was developed through a simple and accessible preparation strategy. The obtained CDs exhibited a fluorescence “turn-off” response to Cu2+ with high selectivity and sensitivity. CDs also achieved imaging Cu2+ in HeLa cell and zebrafish successfully due to its excellent optical performance. This paper provides an idea for carbon nanomaterials to detect life-related substances at the level of organelles and furnish important information for the early diagnosis of related diseases.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declared that they have no conflicts of interest to this work. We declare that we do not have any commercial or associative interest that represents a conflict of interest in connection with the work submitted.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21177061) and Fundamental Research Funds of Jiangsu Key Lab. of Biomass Energy and Material (JSBEM-S-201806).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2021.113143.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
